extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) is VIOLATED. Transition t5 is the culprit: "on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 10): sent := true". This sets sent=true precisely in a state where opted_out is true, directly contradicting the property — provided the guard (opted_out and ticks>=10) is reachable, which it is.

Reachability and counterexample (12 events, within the 14-event bound):
1. optout (t1, always enabled): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
2-11. tick x10 (t4): each tick increments ticks while <10 and sets sent=false. ticks goes 0->1->2->...->10. opted_out stays true. After the 10th tick: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=10.
12. send_attempt: now opted_out=true AND ticks>=10, so t5 is enabled and fires, setting sent=true.

Resulting state after event 12: opted_out=true and sent=true. This is a reachable state where opted_out holds but sent is NOT false, violating "opted_out implies not sent."

Note on rule interaction: on send_attempt with opted_out true and ticks>=10, both t3 (sent:=false) and t5 (sent:=true) have satisfied guards. Even if this is treated as nondeterministic, the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution, so the existence of an execution in which t5 fires suffices to falsify the global property. t5 is effectively a higher-priority/override path (the only reason to add it over t3 is to let sends through after the tick threshold), and it breaks the opt-out invariant. The other sent-setting transition, t2, requires not opted_out and is therefore safe.
